column7: Typo – Because the compound explicitly retains the inflected element kale, it is structurally an aluk-samāsa (a compound that bypasses the typical elision of internal case endings) preserving the locative singular termination.

Typo – It should absolutely be changed to just "day." Defining a kalpa as a "day and night of BRAHMĀ" is a clear cosmological and mathematical error in the Shabda-Sagara (inherited from Horace Hayman Wilson's 1819 dictionary).

Typo – It should be changed to "everything" (as a single word). LB In modern standard English, "everything" is the correct pronoun to mean all things or all instances. LB The appearance of "every thing" as two words in the Shabda-Sagara entry for kalpadruma is simply a typographic relic of 19th-century printing conventions (faithfully digitized from Horace Hayman Wilson's original 1819 text). In that era, terms like every thing, any thing, and some thing were routinely spaced as two distinct words. Modernizing it to a single word is the correct fix for standard readability and searchability.

column5: sUd  sUd  [Printed book page 1698-2]  sUd  I. 1 Ā. <!!!>(sUdte)<!!!> 1 To strike, hurt, wound, kill, destroy. 2 To effuse, pour out. 3 To deposit.

column7: Typo – Also, please note that the marking: "{sic}", may be removed from the Window Display.

Typo – The form "cikitti" does not exist in valid classical or Vedic verbal paradigms; where it appears in digital editions or online search results, it is almost invariably an optical character recognition (OCR) typo for a desiderative stem
